sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.content: Risk factors for small bowel adenocarcinoma include hereditary genetic syndromes (Lynch syndrome with lifetime risk 4% and relative risk >100; Familial adenomatous polyposis with lifetime risk 3-5%; Peutz-Jeghers syndrome with relative risk 520 and lifetime risk 1.7-13%), inflammatory conditions (Inflammatory bowel disease with relative risk 3.70; Celiac disease with 0.06% within 1 year), and lifestyle factors (high alcohol consumption, smoking, dietary factors, elevated BMI) with moderate to weak associations.

cards.strength: Strong. Lifetime risk of SBA is estimated at 4%, representing a relative risk of >100 compared to the general population [15, 16].
cards.detail: An autosomal dominant condition resulting from germline mutations in DNA mismatch repair (MMR) genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2). Carriers have a significantly elevated lifetime risk of developing SBA.

cards.strength: Strong. The lifetime risk of developing SBA has been estimated at 3% to 5% [22, 23].
cards.detail: An autosomal dominant condition characterized by a germline APC gene mutation, leading to numerous adenomatous polyps in the large bowel. The duodenum and periampullary region are the most common sites for SBA in these patients.

cards.strength: Very strong. The relative risk of developing SBA is estimated at 520 compared to the general population, with a lifetime risk between 1.7% and 13% [24, 25, 26].
cards.detail: An autosomal dominant condition mainly caused by STK11 (LKB1) gene mutations, characterized by hamartomatous and adenomatous gastrointestinal polyps. SBA can arise from either type of polyp.

cards.strength: Moderate to Strong. A retrospective cohort study found the relative risk of small bowel cancer in IBD patients was 3.70 (95% CI, 1.23–11.13) [27, 28, 29, 30, 31].
cards.detail: Both ulcerative colitis and Crohn’s disease are recognized risk factors. IBD increases the risk of both colorectal and small bowel cancers.

cards.strength: Moderate. A cohort study found 0.06% of patients with celiac disease were diagnosed with SBA within 1 year of diagnosis, suggesting a possible association [32].
cards.detail: An immune reaction to gluten that damages the small intestine. The link to SBA is less understood than the link between IBD and CRC.
A systematic review reported that high levels of alcohol consumption may increase the risk of SBA. Pooled data showed a non-significant trend towards increased risk with high alcohol consumption.
A systematic review identified smoking as a factor that may increase SBA risk, though a pooled analysis did not identify it as a risk factor.
Low intake of fiber and high intake of red/processed meat and sugary drinks have been reported to increase the risk of SBA in a systematic review.
A pooled analysis showed elevated BMI was associated with a non-significant trend towards increased SBA risk.
